selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":22,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"homosexual","occupation":"waiter/waitress","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":0,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","board games","knitting","cooking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":12,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":38,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":11,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

INPUT:
mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
persona = {"age":22,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"homosexual","occupation":"waiter/waitress","city_of_living":"New York","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":0,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly one hour daily on social media","hobbies":["hiking","board games","knitting","cooking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":12,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":38,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":9,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":11,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I think it's important for everyone, especially young people like me, to be able to talk about these kinds of topics without getting overwhelmed. I've been a waiter here in New York for two years now, which means I'm around all types of customers every day—some are interested in health stuff, others just want to complain or argue—and it's not always easy dealing with that kind of conversation.

I remember one time during lunch rush, there was this group of tourists who kept asking me about the vaccines. They were skeptical and wanted proof everything was okay because they heard some scary things online while visiting other places where people didn't get vaccinated properly or had weird reactions.

As someone with a lower secondary education but raised by parents with PhDs and master's degrees, I have to admit sometimes I feel like an amateur talking about this stuff. But at the same time, my dad actually worked in public health before he retired so maybe there's some wisdom from him that I can recall?

Anyway, what helped me most was keeping calm because according to Big Five personality test results (extraversion score moderate), being too nervous wouldn't help anyway.

I usually start by listening carefully and not jumping straight into arguments unless asked directly. It shows respect and gives people a chance to think instead of getting defensive right away.

Then I try sharing simple facts without overwhelming anyone with technical details. My dad taught me that sometimes people just need reassurance more than complex explanations.

For example, if someone says "I heard vaccines can give you COVID", then it's better to say calmly and clearly that there are many studies showing they're safe, not dangerous.

Also, because I spend about an hour daily on social media myself (and sometimes see misinformation), being able to spot fake news helps too. But my mental health is good so hopefully I won't get anxious during these chats!

Another thing: even though most people don't know me personally and think New York is just a job location, the fact that I'm in this city with all its diversity means we can learn from each other's experiences.

But sometimes when dealing with vaccine discussions online or at work, having disagreements requires finding common ground. Since my agreeableness score was low anyway (score 9), maybe it helps me being direct without burning bridges.

Perhaps sharing personal stories is the best approach—like how I got vaccinated myself because that feels more authentic than just reciting official guidelines.

I also enjoy board games and knitting at home, which means when I'm not dealing with these discussions anymore, I can relax in ways that help keep my mind clear. That's probably why my neuroticism score is low too (11).",
  "reasoning_summary": "To manage healthcare discussions including COVID vaccines effectively, especially given limited formal education but exposure to scientific backgrounds through parents, focus on maintaining composure and sharing accessible information while leveraging personal experiences.",
  "tone": "Calm yet direct with a touch of approachability",
  "citations": ["My father's work in public health", "Various studies cited during conversation"],
  "used_persona_fields": [
    "age",
    "gender",
    "occupation",
    "city_of_living",
    "education_level",
    "parents_education",
    "time_onsocialmedia"
  ]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I think it's important for everyone, especially young people like me, to be able to talk about these kinds of topics without getting overwhelmed. I've been a waiter here in New York for two years now, which means I'm around all types of customers every day—some are interested in health stuff, others just want to complain or argue—and it's not always easy dealing with that kind of conversation.

I remember one time during lunch rush, there was this group of tourists who kept asking me about the vaccines. They were skeptical and wanted proof everything was okay because they heard some scary things online while visiting other places where people didn't get vaccinated properly or had weird reactions.

As someone with a lower secondary education but raised by parents with PhDs and master's degrees, I have to admit sometimes I feel like an amateur talking about this stuff. But at the same time, my dad actually worked in public health before he retired so maybe there's some wisdom from him that I can recall?

Anyway, what helped me most was keeping calm because according to Big Five personality test results (extraversion score moderate), being too nervous wouldn't help anyway.

I usually start by listening carefully and not jumping straight into arguments unless asked directly. It shows respect and gives people a chance to think instead of getting defensive right away.

Then I try sharing simple facts without overwhelming anyone with technical details. My dad taught me that sometimes people just need reassurance more than complex explanations.

For example, if someone says "I heard vaccines can give you COVID", then it's better to say calmly and clearly that there are many studies showing they're safe, not dangerous.

Also, because I spend about an hour daily on social media myself (and sometimes see misinformation), being able to spot fake news helps too. But my mental health is good so hopefully I won't get anxious during these chats!

Another thing: even though most people don't know me personally and think New York is just a job location, the fact that I'm in this city with all its diversity means we can learn from each other's experiences.

But sometimes when dealing with vaccine discussions online or at work, having disagreements requires finding common ground. Since my agreeableness score was low anyway (score 9), maybe it helps me being direct without burning bridges.

Perhaps sharing personal stories is the best approach—like how I got vaccinated myself because that feels more authentic than just reciting official guidelines.

I also enjoy board games and knitting at home, which means when I'm not dealing with these discussions anymore, I can relax in ways that help keep my mind clear. That's probably why my neuroticism score is low too (11).
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: To manage healthcare discussions including COVID vaccines effectively, especially given limited formal education but exposure to scientific backgrounds through parents, focus on maintaining composure and sharing accessible information while leveraging personal experiences.
response_parsed.tone: Calm yet direct with a touch of approachability
citations: My father's work in public health
